coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green emitted carbon dots from Vachellia nilotica and utilizing its extract as a red emitted fluorescence reagent: Applying for visual and spectroscopic detection of iron (III)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Salem AlSalem, Huda
Saad Binkadem, Mona
Talal Al-Goul, Soha
Abdel-Lateef, Mohamed A.
coredata.dc:description: Principles of Green Analytical Chemistry recommended preferring using reagents from renewable sources and eliminating toxic reagents. Vachellia nilotica is a widespread plant throughout different parts of the world. In this study, using microwave energy, fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized for the first time from Vachellia nilotica pods. The morphology of the prepared carbon dots was characterized by SEM and TEM techniques, and the spectroscopic character exhibit green emission at 480 nm at λex = 386.5 nm. This fluorescence can be effectively quenched by adding Fe (III) ions (Method I). Furthermore, Vachellia nilotica pods were extracted by different solvents, including methanol, deionized water, absolute ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, and DMF. The acetonitrile extract of Vachellia nilotica exhibited a strong red fluorescence emission at 673.9 at λex = 410 nm. Among various types of salt metals, only Fe (III) can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the acetonitrile extract (method II). Moreover, the bright yellow color of the aqueous extract can be changed into violet color. The absorbance of the resulted color can be spectrophotometrically measured at λ max = 530 nm (method III). The best analytical factors were optimized for the developed methods. The developed methods were applied to determine Fe (III) in different water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vachellia Nilotica
Carbon dots
Green analytical chemistry
Fe (III) ions
Fluorescence detection
Water analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectroscopic methods for detection of Fe (III)
Using V. Nilotica extract as reagent (Method II)
Determination of iron (III) in water samples
Spectroscopical properties of V. Nilotica extract and C-dots
Morphological characterization for the synthesized C-dots
Detection of Fe (III) by different spectroscopic techniques

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project number RI-44-0298

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra for acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods (B) Fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra for C-dots and, (C) images for C-dots(I), and acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods (II) under daylight, and (D) images for C-dots (I) and acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods (II) under UV lamp, (F) spectrophotometric spectra for aqueous extract of V. nilotica pods and C-dot.
Morphological characterization of the prepared C-dots: (A) for TEM image and (B) for SEM image.

Detection Fe (III) by different spectroscopic methods; (A) Quenching the emission fluorescence spectra of acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods and C-dots by Fe (III), (B) generating a new absorbance peak at λmax = 530 nm after the addition of Fe (III) to aqueous extract of V. nilotica pods.

(A) Selectivity for the detection of Fe (III) (5.0 and 20 µg/mL) by the proposed spectrofluorimetric and spectrophotometric methods using extracts of V. nilotica pods, (B) choosing the best diluting solvent for the proposed methods.
Analytical parameters for the spectroscopic methods.
Detection of Fe (III) in different samples by the proposed spectroscopic methods.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green emitted carbon dots from Vachellia nilotica and utilizing its extract as a red emitted fluorescence reagent: Applying for visual and spectroscopic detection of iron (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Assiut Branch, Assiut 71524, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Assiut Branch, Assiut 71524, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Principles of Green Analytical Chemistry recommended preferring using reagents from renewable sources and eliminating toxic reagents. Vachellia nilotica is a widespread plant throughout different parts of the world. In this study, using microwave energy, fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized for the first time from Vachellia nilotica pods. The morphology of the prepared carbon dots was characterized by SEM and TEM techniques, and the spectroscopic character exhibit green emission at 480 nm at λex = 386.5 nm. This fluorescence can be effectively quenched by adding Fe (III) ions (Method I). Furthermore, Vachellia nilotica pods were extracted by different solvents, including methanol, deionized water, absolute ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, and DMF. The acetonitrile extract of Vachellia nilotica exhibited a strong red fluorescence emission at 673.9 at λex = 410 nm. Among various types of salt metals, only Fe (III) can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the acetonitrile extract (method II). Moreover, the bright yellow color of the aqueous extract can be changed into violet color. The absorbance of the resulted color can be spectrophotometrically measured at λ max = 530 nm (method III). The best analytical factors were optimized for the developed methods. The developed methods were applied to determine Fe (III) in different water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Green Chemistry uses methodologies and techniques that minimize, reduce or exclude using or outputting reagents, products, feedstocks, etc., that are toxic or hazardous to health or the environment [1]. Principles of Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) recommends preferring reagents from renewable sources and eliminating toxic reagents [2,3]. Therefore, using natural sensors instead the synthetic sensors for detecting analytes is greatly compact with the principles of GAC.

The fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted great interest and attendance from many researchers due to their versatile and many-sided applications in optoelectronics, bio/chemical sensors, eco-friendly nature, and biomedical applications [4–6]. Due to their unique optical characteristics and very slight toxical effect, C-dots have numerous merits compared to traditional fluorescence chemical sensors. For instance, organic fluorescent dyes are readily photobleached and have a narrow range from excitation/ emission spectra[7]. On the opposite side, C-dots are resistant to the photobleaching process and wavelength-dependent emission spectra [8]. Numerous synthetic approaches using a variety of methodologies have been devised to synthesize C-dots, such as thermal treatment, hydrothermal treatments, and microwave-assisted [7]. The microwave-assisted processing gives efficient and intensive energy, homogenous heating, and effectively reduces reaction time, which is compact with principles of GAC in lowering energy consumption [2,3,7,9,10].

Natural reagents (such as extracts of plants) have been used in attempts to create more environmentally friendly and greener chemical analyses [11]. Acacia nilotica or Vachellia nilotica (V. nilotica), known as Egyptian thorn tree in Egypt, the thorn in mimosa in Australia, scanted tree in English, and “Bagaruwa” among the Hausas, is a genus of Acacia [12]. It is widespread and distributed throughout different parts of the world, mainly in several regions of the Middle East, Australia, Africa, and India [12,13]. V. nilotica has been utilized in traditional medicine in many African countries (e.g., Sudan, Zimbabwe, Kenya), India, and Pakistan for treating toothache, children’s fevers, eye complaints, sexually transmitted infections, respiratory diseases, enteric ailments, and bacterial, fungal and viral infections [13–16]. Pods of V. nilotica are frequently used due to their higher tannins contents (both hydrolyzable and condensable tannins)[17]. Rather et al exploited the intrinsic native fluorescence properties of polyphenols in V. nilotica extract and utilized it as a natural dye for pigmentation the fiber wool [18].

Iron is a physiologically vital metal due to its existence in numerous types of metalloenzymes and metalloproteins[19]. In spite of its presence in trace amounts in the human body, the imbalance in iron ion concentrations in the human body can induce detrimental effects on the function of the body as well as human health [20]. On one side, there is evidence that suggests an increased iron concentration in certain regions of the human brain linked with Parkinson's disease[21]. In contrast, the deficiency of iron in the body, which is medically referred to as anemia, is known to prevent some organs from working as they should, which can lead to poor health and even death at an earlier age[22]. Hence, selective, sensitive methodologies to monitor either of Fe3+ ions are highly desired.

Few methods have been reported using C-dots derived from biomass as a spectrofluorimetric reagent for detecting Fe3+ions [23–26], and certain analytical methods using the natural plant extracts of guava leave, tea drinks, china root, red cabbage, Indian gooseberry, and lead tree) for either visual or spectrophotometric detection of iron III [27–32]. These simple extracts sourced from plant sources for iron detection contain polyphenols compounds [32]. Furthermore, the spectrofluorimetric and spectrophotometric techniques are accurate, sensitive, simple, economical, and lab-available devices [33–35].

Consequently, we aim to utilize the natural extract of V. nilotica pods as a natural renewable reagent for visual, spectrophotometric, and fluorometric detection of iron III. In addition, we prepare C-dots from pods of V. nilotica using microwave-assisted treatment as a green fluorescent reagent for a spectrofluorimetric detection of iron (III) ions.

V. nilotica pods were collected from Assiut agricultural fields (Assiut, Egypt), aluminum chloride, mercuric chloride, lead nitrate, zinc chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, cobalt chloride, barium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, cadmium chloride and nickel chloride, acetone, absolute ethanol, acetonitrile, methanol, DMF were produced by El-Naser Co. Cario, Egypt. Ferric chloride anhydrous was produced by alpha chemika Co., India. Deionized water was used throughout the work. All metals salts were prepared at the concentration of 100 µg/mL.

V. nilotica pods were well-dried and finely powdered. Several weights from the dried powder (the weight of each equals 20 g) were separately transferred into 250-mL conical flasks. Each weight was separately extracted with specific solvent as 100 mL acetone, 100 mL absolute ethanol, DMF, 100 mL acetonitrile, 100 mL 100 mL methanol, and 200 mL deionized water. Flasks were continuously vortexed for 15 min, placed in a dark place for 48 h, and then filtered. Different filtrates were covered with aluminum foils and stored in a refrigerator until use.

All measurements were performed on a UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan) and FS2 spectrofluorometer device (Scinco Co., Korea), for spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric assay, respectively. JSM-IT200 (JEOL Co, Japan) was utilized to take SEM images and determine the morphology of the synthesized C-dots. JEM-100CX II electron microscope (JEOL Co, Japan) was utilized to take TEM images and determine the size of the C-dots. Microwave LG, 1400 W, produced by LG Co. China, was used in the synthesis of C-dots. This work used a vortex device produced by Gemmy Industrial Corp., Taiwan.

Four grams of V. nilotica pod powder were weighed and wetted with deionized water. The past was heated in the microwave device (900 W) for 30–45 min. After cooling the content to 25 °C, the content was extracted with deionized water and filtered via filter paper. The filtrated solution was centrifugated for 20 min at 4000 rpm and purified using a 0.02 μm filter. The filtrate was stored in the refrigerator until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic methods for detection of Fe (III)

ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of volumetric flasks (10-mL), 1.2 mL of C-dots solution and appropriate concentrations from Fe (III) solution were mixed well, and the final volume of flasks was completed up to 10 mL with water. The assay was performed at λem = 480 nm after at λex = 386.5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using V. Nilotica extract as reagent (Method II)
ce:section.ce:para:
Into a series of volumetric flasks (10-mL), 3.0 mL from the acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica was mixed and shaken with appropriate concentrations from Fe (III) solution. The volume of flasks was completed up to 10.0 mL with absolute ethanol. The quenching in the red fluorescence emission of the blank was recorded at λem = 673.9 nm after at λex = 410.5 nm. Finally, The quenching value in fluorescent intensity of the reagent (at λem = 480 nm and λem = 673.9 nm for Method I and Method II, respectively) was calculated as FIo‑FI, where
FI
and FIo refers to the green fluorescence emission intensities of the synthesized C-dots in existence and the omission of Fe (III), respectively, and is used to construct the calibration curve through plotting (FIo‑FI)/FIo against the concentration of Fe (III).

Into a series of volumetric flasks (10-mL), 4.0 mL from the aqueous extract of V. nilotica was mixed and shaken with appropriate concentrations from Fe (III) solution. The volume of flasks was completed up to 10.0 mL with deionized water. Absorbance values of the produced color were measured spectrophotometrically at λ max = 530 nm against the blank solution. Finally, values of the generated absorbance were plotted against values of the increment in the concentration of iron ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of iron (III) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested methods were applied to detect iron (III) in tap water, bottled water, and river water samples. The collected samples were spiked with various known concentrations from iron (III) solution, and the proposed methods were utilized to calculate the total concentrations of Fe (III) in each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopical properties of V. Nilotica extract and C-dots

ce:section.ce:para: V. nilotica pods were extracted with different types of solvents, including water, absolute ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, DMF, and acetone. All solvents extract of V. nilotica pods exhibited good red fluorescence activity (λem = 673.9/ λex = 410.5 nm) except the aqueous extract appeared without any fluorescence strength, Fig. 1 (A). Meanwhile, the acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity over all other types of extracts. On the other side, the aqueous solution for the synthesized C-dots exhibits a green fluorescence activity at λem = 480 upon λex = 386 nm, Fig. 1 (B). Furthermore, the optical examination for acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica and the synthesized C-dots were achieved under daylight and UV lamp. As displayed in Fig. 1 (C & D), the synthesized C-dots exhibit a light brown color and green fluorescence Fig. 1 (C & D, images I), while the acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica pods exhibit a greenish yellow color and red fluorescence Fig. 1 (C & D, images II), under daylight and UV lamp, respectively.

The aqueous extract of V. nilotica exhibit bright yellow color with many absorbance peaks in the UV region of 200–350 nm, while the synthesized C-dots exhibit λmax peak at 260 nm corresponding to π -
π
∗
transition of aromatic system
∁ = ∁
band, which is similar to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon
∁ = ∁
system[36]. While, the shown peak at 380 nm, related to n -
π
∗
transition of
∁ = O
band [37], which agree with excitation wavelength at the fluorescence spectrum of synthesized carbon dots. The UV–Visible spectrum for the aqueous extract of V. nilotica and C-dots are offered in Fig. 1 (F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization for the synthesized C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: TEM in Fig. 2 (A) appears the tiny size of the synthesized C-dots, less than 10 nm. Meanwhile, SEM image in Fig. 2(B) appears the trigonal pyramidal shape of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe (III) by different spectroscopic techniques

ce:section.ce:para:
Method I: Fe (III) ions can effectively quench the green fluorescence emission of C-dot at 480 nm after excitation at 386.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (A). The factors affecting the quenching of fluorescence intensity, such as the volume of C-dots and pH, were studied and optimized. It was noticed the green fluorescence emission of carbon dots was completely absent in the highly alkaline medium, and the high fluorescence activity, as well as the optimum quenching efficacy by Fe (III), was obtained at a pH range from 4.0 to 7.0, so no buffer added and the optimum volume from C-dots was 1.2 mL. Moreover, The quenching efficacy of iron (III) ions on the green fluorescence emission of C-dots was estimated by applying Stern-Volmer analysis [38].
FI
o
FI
=
K
SV
Q
+ 1
where
FI
and FIo refer to the green fluorescence emission intensities of the synthesized carbon dots in existence and the omission of Fe (III), respectively, [Q] is the concentration of Fe (III), and Ksv is the quenching constant. It was found that the ratio (FI o /FI) in linear correlation state (r = 0.9928) with iron (III) ions concentration, with Stern–Volmer quenching constant of
K
SV
= 42378
M
- 1
. The linear range between the decrease in the green fluorescence intensities of the synthesized C-dots represented as (FIo
- FI)/FIo and Fe (III) concentration was 0.1 - 4.0 µg/mL with the equation of (FIo‑FI)/FIo
= 0.128C + 0.0306, and 0.9989 and 0.9978 values for r and r2, respectively.

Method II: acetonitrile extract of V. nilotica exhibits a strong red fluorescence emission at 673.9 nm after an excitation at 410.5 nm. This red fluorescence emission can be effectively quenched upon adding iron (III) ions, as shown in Fig. 3(A). Different types of metals, including aluminum chloride, mercuric chloride, lead nitrate, zinc chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, cobalt chloride, barium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, cadmium chloride, and nickel chloride were tested without producing any effect on the red fluorescence-emission intensity of V. nilotica extract, as shown in Fig. 4 (A). The optimum quenching was achieved by optimizing the volume of the natural reagent, the pH of the medium, and the diluting solvent. The optimum conditions were obtained by using 3.0 mL from the acetonitrile extract at a pH value of 7.0 without buffer (drastic alkaline destroyed the fluorescence, and the acidic medium diminished it) and the use of ethanol or DMF as diluting solvent. Ethanol is considered a green and renewable solvent [39]; therefore, the chosen diluting solvent in procedures was ethanol, Fig. 4(B). The linear range between the decrease in the red fluorescence intensity of V. nilotica extract represented as (FIo
- FI)/FIo, and Fe (III) concentration was 0.25 - 7.0 µg/mL with the equation of (FIo
- F)/FIo
= 0.0396C + 0.0157, and 0.999 and 0.998 values for r and r2, respectively. Moreover, the statistical parameters for Method I and Method II are offered in Table 1 .

All extracts of V. nilotica were tested to determine the best results that can change the color of V. nilotica extracts from yellow to violet. It was found that the highest degree of violet color formation was obtained with the aqueous extract of V. nilotica pods, followed by the acetonitrile extract. The formed color was measured spectrophotometrically at λmax = 530 nm, and the naked eye can observe the formed violet color. Others metals salts such as aluminum chloride, mercuric chloride, lead nitrate, zinc chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, cobalt chloride, barium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, cadmium chloride, and nickel chloride did not produce any effect on the yellow color of V. nilotica aqueous extraction as shown in Fig. 4(A). Furthermore, the best absorbance was achieved by using 4.0 mL from the aqueous extract, pH value of 7.0 i.e. without buffer (drastic alkaline increases the background of blank and no color formation in the drastic acidic medium) and using water (the best green solvent) as diluting solvent, Fig. 4(B). The linear relationship between the increase in the absorbance at 530 nm and Fe (III) concentration was 2.5 - 30.0 µg/mL with the equation of absorbance = 0.0226C + 0.0131, and 0.999 and 0.998 values for r and r2, respectively. Additionally, statistical parameters of method III are presented in Table 1.

To assess the practicality, the suggested spectroscopic methods were applied to determine Fe (III) in different types of water samples, including bottled water and river water. All the examined samples were centrifuged and filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane before the analysis. The obtained results for detecting Fe (III) are offered in Table 2 .

Based on the utilization of spectroscopical properties of the natural biomass as a green analytical reagent, two spectrofluorimetric methods and one spectrophotometric method were developed to detect Fe (III) ions. The green fluorescence reagents were prepared either through extraction V. nilotica pods to obtain a red emitted fluorescence reagent at λem = 673.9 nm / λex = 410.5 nm or through carbonization of V. nilotica pods to obtain a green emitted fluorescence reagent at λem = 480 nm / λex = 386.5 nm. Fe (III) ions exhibited an efficient quenching capacity on both types of fluorescence. Furthermore, Fe (III) ions can change the bright yellow color of the aqueous extract of V. nilotica to violet color with the appearance of a new absorbance peak at 530 nm to develop a green spectrophotometric method for a renewable and simple detection of Fe (III) ion.

Huda Salem AlSalem: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Writing – review & editing. Mona Saad Binkadem: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Software, Writing – review & editing. Soha Talal Al-Goul: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed A. Abdel-Lateef: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project number RI-44-0298
